name: Mojiang Mine Incident
description: The Mojiang Mine Incident refers to a cluster of severe pneumonia cases that occurred in 2012 among six miners working in a bat-infested copper mine in Mojiang County, Yunnan Province, China. These miners were tasked with clearing bat guano from the mine. All six developed severe respiratory illnesses, and three subsequently died. While mainstream science attributes the illnesses and deaths to fungal infections (specifically, *Histoplasma capsulatum*) acquired from the bat guano, alternative theories suggest a more complex or even engineered origin, potentially linked to viral research or bioweapons development. Proponents of these theories point to the unusual severity and rapid progression of the illnesses, as well as the subsequent research conducted on bat coronaviruses in the region, particularly at the Wuhan Institute of Virology. They argue that the Mojiang miners may have been exposed to a novel, highly virulent virus. Mainstream science refutes these claims, emphasizing that the symptoms were consistent with histoplasmosis, a known risk associated with bat guano exposure. Furthermore, the genetic analysis of coronaviruses found in the Mojiang mine bats, including RaTG13 (genetically closest known relative to SARS-CoV-2), does not directly link them to the miners' illnesses. Critics also highlight the lack of concrete evidence supporting any bioweapons or engineered virus narrative, attributing the alternative theories to speculation and misinterpretation of scientific data.
subtitle: Mysterious Illnesses in a Chinese Mine

claims.claim: The miners were exposed to a novel, highly virulent virus.
claims.evidence: The rapid and severe onset of respiratory illnesses in all six miners, and the subsequent research on bat coronaviruses in the same mine.
claims.criticism: Mainstream science attributes the illnesses to histoplasmosis, a fungal infection common in bat guano. Genetic analysis of coronaviruses found in the mine does not directly link them to the miners' illnesses.

claims.claim: The illnesses were caused by a deliberately engineered bioweapon or virus.
claims.evidence: The close proximity of the Mojiang mine to the Wuhan Institute of Virology, and the fact that researchers collected samples from the mine.
claims.criticism: There is no concrete evidence to support the claim of a bioweapon. The Wuhan Institute of Virology's research on bat coronaviruses is publicly documented and aimed at understanding and preventing future outbreaks.

claims.claim: The Mojiang mine incident is connected to the origins of the COVID-19 pandemic.
claims.evidence: The RaTG13 coronavirus, found in the Mojiang mine bats, is genetically the closest known relative to SARS-CoV-2. Some believe this suggests a potential link.
claims.criticism: While RaTG13 is the closest known relative, it is still significantly different from SARS-CoV-2, and there is no direct evidence linking it to the pandemic. Furthermore, the genetic gap between RaTG13 and SARS-CoV-2 suggests considerable evolutionary divergence.

keyFigures.name: Peter Daszak
keyFigures.role: President of EcoHealth Alliance, which collaborated with the Wuhan Institute of Virology on bat coronavirus research.

keyFigures.name: Shi Zhengli
keyFigures.role: Virologist at the Wuhan Institute of Virology, known for her research on bat coronaviruses.

keyFigures.name: Alina Chan
keyFigures.role: Molecular biologist who has argued for the possibility of a lab leak origin of COVID-19, referencing the Mojiang mine incident.

mythologyConnections: Connections to traditional Chinese beliefs about caves and hidden realms.
Possible parallels to stories of curses or plagues originating from underground sources.

timeline.event: Six miners develop severe pneumonia after clearing bat guano in the Mojiang mine.
Three miners die from the illness.
Wuhan Institute of Virology researchers collect samples from the Mojiang mine and identify RaTG13.
The COVID-19 pandemic begins, leading to increased scrutiny of bat coronavirus research and the Mojiang mine incident.

sources.author: Bloom, J. D.
sources.work: Recovery of a deleted deep mutational scanning dataset sheds important light on the receptor binding domain of SARS-CoV-2's spike protein
sources.title: Bloom, J. D.
